title: Cottage pie
subtitle: Beef mince with vegetables and gravy under creamy mash
excerpt: Minced beef cooked with onion, carrots, celery, and stock into a rich gravy, topped with mashed potato and baked until golden. The beef version of shepherd's pie. A reliable weeknight supper and a Sunday table staple across British households.
sourceNotes: Cottage pie uses beef; shepherd's pie uses lamb. Both follow the same method. The name 'cottage pie' appears in English cookbooks from the 18th century, associated with the economical use of minced meat. The dish became a staple of British institutional cooking and school dinner culture in the 20th century, which has not diminished its popularity at home.

recipe.freezeNotes: Assemble and freeze before baking. Defrost fully, then bake adding 10 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Make two at once. The mince base scales directly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the mince filling up to 2 days ahead, refrigerate, top with mash, and bake when needed.

content.text: Brown the mince properly. Pale, steamed mince produces a grey, flat filling. Get the pan very hot, add the mince in batches if needed, and do not stir until a crust has formed on the bottom. The Maillard reaction in the mince is the primary source of flavour in the filling.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large pan over medium heat. Cook the {{onion}}, {{carrot}}, and {{celery}} for 10 minutes until softened. Increase to high heat, add the {{beef-mince}}, and cook without stirring for 3 minutes, then break it up and continue cooking until browned all over. Add the {{tomato-puree}} and {{thyme}}. Stir for 2 minutes. Add the {{worcestershire-sauce}} and {{stock-beef}}. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Simmer uncovered for 20 minutes until the sauce is thick. Transfer to a baking dish.

Heat the oven to 190°C. Boil the {{potato}} in salted water until completely tender. Drain and steam-dry. Mash with {{unsalted-butter}} and warm {{whole-milk}} until smooth. Season well. Spread over the filling, rough up the surface with a fork, and bake for 25 to 30 minutes until golden on top and bubbling at the edges.

items.cause: Mince not properly browned, or cooked at too low a temperature
items.fix: High heat and patience before stirring. The mince should form a brown crust before you break it up

items.cause: Stock too salty before adding worcestershire sauce
items.fix: Use low-sodium stock or homemade. Season at the end rather than the beginning, as the sauce concentrates during the simmer

content.text: Cottage pie is eaten on weeknights across Britain, more so than shepherd's pie because beef mince is more commonly bought than lamb mince. It reheats well, improves over two days, and feeds a family economically. The mash topping ensures it does not need any accompaniment beyond peas or a simple green vegetable.
